cuisine: Global / Multi-regional (South Asian, Middle Eastern, Latin American, Southeast Asian)
region_of_origin: Middle East / South Asia (widely cultivated across Iran, India, and the Mediterranean)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of cumin seeds is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 3g), used as a whole spice in tempering, curries, rice dishes, or spice blends.

nutrient_source.title: Spices, cumin seed — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID: 170923)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw whole cumin seeds used as primary nutrient source. Input HPB values cross-referenced; energy (375 kcal), protein (17.81g), fat (22.27g), carbohydrate (44.24g) are consistent with USDA data (375 kcal, 17.81g, 22.27g, 44.24g). HPB values retained as they align with USDA SR Legacy within rounding tolerance. Saturated fat sourced from USDA (1.535g/100g). Potassium (1788mg), calcium (931mg), iron (66.36mg) from HPB/USDA cross-reference.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Spices and Condiments — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for whole spices and high-fibre seeds
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for cumin seeds exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 35 (low) based on: (1) high dietary fibre content (10.5g/100g) which substantially attenuates glycaemic response; (2) analogous whole spice seeds with similar fibre and carbohydrate profiles in the University of Sydney database; (3) peer-reviewed literature (Zare et al., 2014, Complementary Therapies in Clinical Practice) documenting cumin's hypoglycaemic properties and low glycaemic impact. Glycemic load per 3g serving = (35 × 1.33g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.5, classified as negligible. This is an analogous estimate and should be updated if a direct measurement becomes available.

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium-to-sodium ratio (1788mg K : 168mg Na per 100g) and high dietary fibre (10.5g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Cumin seeds have an exceptionally favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio supporting DASH diet principles for blood pressure management; at typical culinary serving sizes of 1–5g, sodium contribution is negligible (approximately 1.7–8.4mg per serving) while potassium and fibre provide vascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycaemic index (estimated ~35), high dietary fibre (10.5g/100g), and evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cumin seeds are appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; their high fibre content slows glucose absorption, and peer-reviewed evidence (Zare et al., 2014) supports improved insulin sensitivity and fasting blood glucose reduction with regular cumin consumption at culinary doses.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.54g/100g), dominant unsaturated fat profile (monounsaturated 14.04g, polyunsaturated 3.28g), zero cholesterol, and high potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cumin seeds have a cardioprotective lipid profile with very low saturated fat and zero cholesterol; their high potassium content (1788mg/100g) and anti-inflammatory polyphenols support arterial health, and at typical spice-level servings they contribute negligible saturated fat to the diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polyphenols, negligible saturated fat, and high dietary fibre
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cumin seeds contain anti-inflammatory bioactive compounds including cuminaldehyde and flavonoids; their negligible saturated fat content and high fibre qualify them as an anti-inflammatory spice suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis at culinary serving sizes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cumin seeds (Cuminum cyminum) are small, oblong, ridged seeds with a warm, earthy, slightly bitter and nutty flavour profile, widely used as a whole or ground spice across South Asian, Middle Eastern, Latin American, North African, and Southeast Asian cuisines. Raw dried cumin seeds are minimally processed, retaining their full nutritional profile. They are used in tempering (tadka) in Indian cooking, as a key component of spice blends such as garam masala, cumin rice (jeera rice), and curry powders, and as a flavouring in Mexican dishes, hummus, and Moroccan tagines. Nutritionally, cumin seeds are exceptionally nutrient-dense per 100g: they provide 375 kcal, 17.81g protein, 22.27g fat (predominantly unsaturated), and 44.24g carbohydrate with 10.5g dietary fibre. They are extraordinarily rich in iron (66.36mg/100g — among the highest of any food), calcium (931mg/100g), and potassium (1788mg/100g). Their saturated fat content is very low (1.54g/100g) and cholesterol is zero. Clinically, cumin seeds are suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (low GI, high fibre, evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties), hypertension (exceptional potassium-to-sodium ratio), and cardiovascular disease (cardioprotective lipid profile). At typical culinary serving sizes of 1–5g, the absolute nutrient contribution is modest but the bioactive compound delivery (cuminaldehyde, flavonoids, terpenoids) is clinically meaningful. Cumin seeds are vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: How much iron is in cumin seeds?
Are cumin seeds good for blood pressure?
Is cumin seeds high in fibre?
Can I eat cumin seeds if I have hypertension?
What is the glycemic index of cumin seeds?
Are cumin seeds vegan and gluten-free?

ingredient_synonyms.jeera: Hindi/Urdu common name for cumin seeds

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values from HPB Singapore are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID: 170923) for raw cumin seeds within rounding tolerance. Both are approved primary sources. Micronutrient values cross-referenced between HPB and USDA. GI is an analogous estimate (no direct measurement available) documented with peer-reviewed support. Ontology codes verified against FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT.
notes: 1. Cholesterol is 0.0mg/100g — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and HPB; cumin seeds are a plant-derived food that naturally contains no cholesterol. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 and Vitamin D is 0.0 — naturally zero as these are plant-based seeds with no animal-derived components. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy. 2. Iron value of 66.36mg/100g is exceptionally high and is consistent with both HPB and USDA SR Legacy data for dried cumin seeds; this reflects the concentrated mineral content of dried spice seeds and is not an error. 3. GI of approximately 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct University of Sydney GI Database measurement exists for cumin seeds. Estimate is defensible based on high fibre content and peer-reviewed hypoglycaemic literature (Zare et al., 2014). 4. Saturated fat (1.54g), monounsaturated fat (14.04g), polyunsaturated fat (3.28g), and micronutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170923 to supplement HPB data. 5. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' from the input has been removed as cumin seeds are not a grain; 'high_fat_warning' has been contextualised — total fat is 22.27g/100g but is predominantly unsaturated and consumed in small culinary quantities. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g all populated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170923.
